04 Prototypes & Classes — The Editorial Chain of Command

← Back to lessons

JavaScript does not have classical inheritance. It has prototypal delegation. The class keyword is syntactic sugar over a fundamentally different system. Understanding the real mechanism is the difference between copying patterns and designing them.

The Mental Model: Editorial Chain of Command

In a newsroom, when a junior reporter has a question they can't answer, they ask their editor. If the editor doesn't know, they ask the managing editor. If the managing editor doesn't know, they ask the editor-in-chief. The question travels up the chain until someone has the answer or the chain ends.

JavaScript objects work identically. When you access a property on an object, if the object doesn't have it, JavaScript asks the object's prototype. If the prototype doesn't have it, JavaScript asks the prototype's prototype. This chain continues until it reaches null (the end of the chain).

This is not inheritance. It's delegation. The object doesn't "have" its prototype's methods. It delegates to the prototype when asked. The distinction matters: in classical inheritance, subclasses get copies. In prototypal delegation, objects share live references up the chain.

The Prototype Chain

const reporter = {
  file(story) {
    console.log(`Filing: ${story}`);
  }
};

const trevor = Object.create(reporter);
trevor.name = "Trevor";
trevor.beat = "politics";

trevor.file("Election results");
// "Filing: Election results"
// trevor doesn't have `file` — it delegates to reporter

trevor.hasOwnProperty("name");  // true  (own property)
trevor.hasOwnProperty("file");  // false (on the prototype)

The chain for trevor:

trevor → reporter → Object.prototype → null

// trevor.file       → found on reporter
// trevor.toString   → found on Object.prototype
// trevor.foo        → not found anywhere → undefined

Inspecting the Chain

// Get an object's prototype
Object.getPrototypeOf(trevor) === reporter;  // true

// The older, non-standard way (still widely used)
trevor.__proto__ === reporter;  // true

// Check if something is in the chain
reporter.isPrototypeOf(trevor);  // true

Constructor Functions (The Pre-Class Way)

Before class, this was how you created "types" in JavaScript:

function Reporter(name, beat) {
  this.name = name;
  this.beat = beat;
}

// Methods go on the prototype (shared across all instances)
Reporter.prototype.file = function(story) {
  console.log(`${this.name} filing: ${story}`);
};

const trevor = new Reporter("Trevor", "politics");
const sarah = new Reporter("Sarah", "tech");

trevor.file("Election day");  // "Trevor filing: Election day"
sarah.file("AI launch");      // "Sarah filing: AI launch"

// Both share the SAME function object
trevor.file === sarah.file;   // true

What new Actually Does

The new keyword does exactly 4 things:

// new Reporter("Trevor", "politics") is equivalent to:

// 1. Create a new empty object
const obj = {};

// 2. Set its prototype to Reporter.prototype
Object.setPrototypeOf(obj, Reporter.prototype);

// 3. Call Reporter with `this` bound to the new object
Reporter.call(obj, "Trevor", "politics");

// 4. Return the object (unless the constructor returns an object)
return obj;

Common mistake: Forgetting new. Without it, this inside the constructor points to the global object (or undefined in strict mode). This is why the convention is to capitalize constructor function names — it's a visual signal that new is required.

Class Syntax: Sugar, Not Magic

ES6 class is syntactic sugar over the constructor function + prototype pattern. Nothing new at runtime.

Constructor function
function Reporter(name) {
  this.name = name;
}

Reporter.prototype.file = function(s) {
  console.log(`${this.name}: ${s}`);
};

const t = new Reporter("Trevor");
Class syntax (identical behavior)
class Reporter {
  constructor(name) {
    this.name = name;
  }

  file(s) {
    console.log(`${this.name}: ${s}`);
  }
}

const t = new Reporter("Trevor");

Under the hood, both produce identical prototype chains:

t → Reporter.prototype → Object.prototype → null

Class Features

class Article {
  // Public field
  status = "draft";

  // Private field (# prefix)
  #wordCount = 0;

  // Static method (on the class itself, not instances)
  static fromMarkdown(md) {
    const article = new Article();
    article.#wordCount = md.split(/\s+/).length;
    return article;
  }

  // Getter
  get words() {
    return this.#wordCount;
  }

  // Setter
  set words(count) {
    if (count < 0) throw new Error("Invalid word count");
    this.#wordCount = count;
  }

  constructor(title) {
    this.title = title;
  }

  publish() {
    this.status = "published";
    console.log(`Published: ${this.title} (${this.#wordCount} words)`);
  }
}

const article = new Article("Breaking News");
article.words = 500;
article.publish();
// "Published: Breaking News (500 words)"

article.#wordCount;  // SyntaxError: Private field

Private fields (#) are truly private. Unlike the closure-based privacy from lesson 03, # fields are enforced by the engine. Even subclasses can't access them. This is one thing class adds that constructor functions can't easily do.

Inheritance with extends

class Reporter {
  constructor(name) {
    this.name = name;
  }

  file(story) {
    console.log(`${this.name} filing: ${story}`);
  }
}

class SeniorReporter extends Reporter {
  constructor(name, years) {
    super(name);  // MUST call super before using `this`
    this.years = years;
  }

  // Override parent method
  file(story) {
    super.file(story);  // Call parent version
    console.log(`(${this.years} years experience)`);
  }

  mentor(junior) {
    console.log(`${this.name} mentoring ${junior.name}`);
  }
}

const senior = new SeniorReporter("Trevor", 8);
senior.file("Investigation");
// "Trevor filing: Investigation"
// "(8 years experience)"

The prototype chain:

senior → SeniorReporter.prototype → Reporter.prototype → Object.prototype → null

Composition Over Inheritance

Deep inheritance hierarchies are fragile. Modern JavaScript (and React) prefers composition:

Inheritance (fragile)
class Animal { ... }
class Dog extends Animal { ... }
class ServiceDog extends Dog { ... }
class GuideDog extends ServiceDog { ... }

// What if you need a Robot Guide?
// It's not an Animal...
// The hierarchy breaks.
Composition (flexible)
const canWalk = (state) => ({
  walk: () => state.position++
});

const canGuide = (state) => ({
  guide: (person) =>
    console.log(`Guiding ${person}`)
});

function createGuideDog(name) {
  const state = { name, position: 0 };
  return {
    ...canWalk(state),
    ...canGuide(state),
  };
}

React made this choice explicitly. React moved from class components (with lifecycle inheritance) to hooks (with composition). useState + useEffect compose better than componentDidMount + componentDidUpdate. Same principle: mix behaviors, don't stack hierarchies.

The this Keyword

this in JavaScript is determined by how a function is called, not where it's defined (the opposite of lexical scope):

const reporter = {
  name: "Trevor",
  greet() {
    console.log(`Hi, I'm ${this.name}`);
  }
};

reporter.greet();          // "Hi, I'm Trevor" (method call → this = reporter)

const greet = reporter.greet;
greet();                   // "Hi, I'm undefined" (plain call → this = global/undefined)

const button = document.createElement('button');
button.addEventListener('click', reporter.greet);
// When clicked: "Hi, I'm " (this = the button element)

Fixing this

// 1. Arrow functions (lexical this — from lesson 03)
const reporter = {
  name: "Trevor",
  greet: () => {
    // Arrow functions don't have their own `this`
    // They use the `this` from the enclosing scope
  }
};

// 2. bind() — creates a new function with fixed `this`
const boundGreet = reporter.greet.bind(reporter);

// 3. Class fields with arrow functions
class Reporter {
  name = "Trevor";
  // Arrow function in a class field — `this` always = instance
  greet = () => console.log(`Hi, I'm ${this.name}`);
}

Knowledge Check

1. What's the difference between obj.hasOwnProperty('x') returning true vs 'x' in obj returning true?

hasOwnProperty only checks the object itself. The in operator checks the entire prototype chain. So 'toString' in {} is true (found on Object.prototype), but {}.hasOwnProperty('toString') is false.

2. Why must you call super() before using this in a subclass constructor?

In a derived class, this is not initialized until super() is called. The parent constructor is responsible for creating the object that this will refer to. Accessing this before super() throws a ReferenceError.

3. Is class just syntactic sugar? What does it add that constructor functions don't have?

Mostly sugar, but with extras: (1) class must be called with new (constructors don't enforce this), (2) methods are non-enumerable by default, (3) class bodies are always in strict mode, (4) private fields (#) have no constructor-function equivalent, (5) extends sets up prototype chains for both instance and static methods.

Exercise: Prototype Chain Detective

1. Create a Newsroom class with a publish(article) method.

2. Create a DigitalNewsroom that extends it and overrides publish to also call this.tweet(article).

3. Use Object.getPrototypeOf() to walk the full prototype chain of a DigitalNewsroom instance and log each level.

4. Verify that both instances share the same toString method (from Object.prototype).

// Your chain should be:
// instance → DigitalNewsroom.prototype → Newsroom.prototype → Object.prototype → null
